behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watch behavior (recursive/subdirectory event delivery), which depends on OS + watcher backend semantics. A mocked watcher can hide real missed subdirectory events. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temporary watched directory tree, create a file in a nested subdirectory, and assert one transformed output is produced without restart.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily module handoff/state behavior (event intake + dedup store + pipeline trigger) and can be caught with in-process wiring plus controllable time. Real OS/network/subprocess is not required by the behavior text. Higher levels are disqualified: no AI/ML output dependency, no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Wire watcher-event handler, dedup component, and pipeline dispatcher in-process with a fake clock; submit two requests for the same normalized path inside the window and assert only one dispatch occurs.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly targets OS-level duplicate filesystem events; this failure appears at real watcher/OS boundary. In-process mocks can miss event burst/timing characteristics. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon with real filesystem watching, perform a single write pattern known to trigger multiple events, and assert exactly one pipeline execution/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This requires real OS filesystem lifecycle semantics (directory deletion/recreation, inode/path rebinding, watcher reattachment). Mocked/in-process tests can hide the rewatch failure. Higher levels are disqualified: no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against a real watched path, delete and recreate that directory while process stays alive, then add a new file and assert it is processed successfully.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real file arrival/event timing plus filesystem timestamp behavior (resolution/metadata retrieval), so real infrastructure is needed to catch true ordering bugs. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create several files in rapid succession in a real watched directory, capture processing order, and assert it matches creation timestamps as read by the daemon’s production metadata path.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This failure mode is tied to real filesystem write/event timing (partial writes, flush/close semantics). Mocking event/write boundaries often hides premature pickup bugs. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real watched directory, write a file in chunks with delays, verify processing does not start mid-write, then confirm processing occurs after final close/complete write.
